preamble: The shift from a retroflex stop [ʈ] (or its voiced counterpart [ɖ]) to a lateral sound is a documented form of lenition. In languages with established retroflex series, intervocalic stops often weaken by allowing airflow around the sides of the tongue, resulting in a retroflex or alveolar lateral.

examples.note: Intervocalic voiced retroflex stops in the Rigveda regularly shifted to retroflex lateral flaps.
Lenition of stops to liquids is a common feature in non-initial positions in many Australian languages.
